title: HTML Comments Contain Sensitive Keywords
description: HTML comments containing sensitive keywords (passwords, API keys, TODO notes).
evidence: Sensitive keywords found in HTML comments.
riskImpact: HTML comments are visible to anyone who views page source.
explanation: Developers often leave comments referencing sensitive systems or credentials.
fixSteps: Remove all comments containing sensitive information.
Use a build step that strips HTML comments.
Move developer notes to server-side files.

codeExamples.label: Webpack
codeExamples.code: new TerserPlugin({ terserOptions: { output: { comments: false } }, extractComments: false })

title: SQL Database Error Messages Exposed
description: Database error messages visible in the page.
evidence: SQL error patterns in page content.
riskImpact: SQL errors reveal database type, version, schema details, and query structure.
explanation: Exposed SQL errors indicate the application may be vulnerable to SQL injection and is leaking infrastructure details.
fixSteps: Implement proper error handling.
Never display raw database errors to users.
Use parameterized queries for all database operations.

codeExamples.label: Parameterized query

title: Timing-Allow-Origin Set to Wildcard
description: Timing-Allow-Origin header is set to '*', allowing any origin to read detailed timing data.
evidence: Timing-Allow-Origin: *
riskImpact: Attackers can use high-resolution timing data from the Resource Timing API to infer information about your application's backend, cache behavior, and authentication state.
explanation: The Timing-Allow-Origin header controls which origins can read timing data via the Resource Timing API. Setting it to '*' gives all sites access to this information.
fixSteps: Restrict Timing-Allow-Origin to specific trusted origins instead of '*'.
Remove the header entirely if cross-origin timing data is not needed.

title: Server header truncated
description: Server: gunicorn/19.9.0 (truncated) — the truncated string indicates a configured but possibly lying value.
evidence: Async / body / header check for server-header-truncated.
riskImpact: Validate that your reverse proxy is not echoing real upstream version
explanation: Server: gunicorn/19.9.0 (truncated) — the truncated string indicates a configured but possibly lying value.
fixSteps: Validate that your reverse proxy is not echoing real upstream version

title: PHP session cookie naming exposes runtime
description: PHPSESSID in cookie name reveals PHP.
evidence: Async / body / header check for php-version-exposed-in-cookie.
riskImpact: Rename the session cookie to a generic opaque value
explanation: PHPSESSID in cookie name reveals PHP.
fixSteps: Rename the session cookie to a generic opaque value

title: Rails version disclosure via cookies
description: _<appname>_session cookie exposes the framework.
evidence: Async / body / header check for rails-version-exposure.
riskImpact: Set a custom session cookie name in config.session_store :key
explanation: _<appname>_session cookie exposes the framework.
fixSteps: Set a custom session cookie name in config.session_store :key

title: Django CSRF cookie name reveals framework
description: csrftoken and django-session cookies disclose the framework.
evidence: Async / body / header check for django-csrftoken-cookie-exposed.
riskImpact: Override CSRF_COOKIE_NAME and SESSION_COOKIE_NAME in settings
explanation: csrftoken and django-session cookies disclose the framework.
fixSteps: Override CSRF_COOKIE_NAME and SESSION_COOKIE_NAME in settings

title: Laravel session cookie exposes framework
description: <app>_session or XSRF-TOKEN cookies disclose Laravel.
evidence: Async / body / header check for laravel-session-cookie-exposes.
riskImpact: Set SESSION_COOKIE and XSRF_COOKIE in config/session.php
explanation: <app>_session or XSRF-TOKEN cookies disclose Laravel.
fixSteps: Set SESSION_COOKIE and XSRF_COOKIE in config/session.php

title: Express connect.sid cookie exposes framework
description: connect.sid is the default Express session cookie name.
evidence: Async / body / header check for express-cookie-exposes.
riskImpact: Pass name: 'sid' (or similar) to express-session
explanation: connect.sid is the default Express session cookie name.
fixSteps: Pass name: 'sid' (or similar) to express-session

title: Rails cookie missing HttpOnly
description: _<app>_session cookie should be HttpOnly.
evidence: Async / body / header check for rails-cookie-httponly.
riskImpact: Set config.session_store :httponly => true
explanation: _<app>_session cookie should be HttpOnly.
fixSteps: Set config.session_store :httponly => true

title: config.js / settings.js leaked
description: Public config.js / settings.js often leaks API keys, environment hints, or feature flags.
evidence: Async / body / header check for config-js-leaked.
riskImpact: Move secrets out of public config files
explanation: Public config.js / settings.js often leaks API keys, environment hints, or feature flags.
fixSteps: Move secrets out of public config files

title: env.js / environment.js exposed
description: env.js in /public/ exposes environment-level secrets.
evidence: Async / body / header check for env-js-leaked.
riskImpact: Never serve env.js from a public path
explanation: env.js in /public/ exposes environment-level secrets.
fixSteps: Never serve env.js from a public path

title: Sitemap.xml publicly accessible
description: Sitemap.xml is publicly accessible by design. Make sure it doesn't reference private endpoints.
evidence: Async / body / header check for sitemap-public.
riskImpact: Audit sitemap.xml for admin / private paths
explanation: Sitemap.xml is publicly accessible by design. Make sure it doesn't reference private endpoints.
fixSteps: Audit sitemap.xml for admin / private paths

title: robots.txt allows everything
description: robots.txt disallows nothing — that's fine but worth noting.
evidence: Async / body / header check for robots-txt-allows-all.
riskImpact: Add explicit Disallow for paths you don't want crawled
explanation: robots.txt disallows nothing — that's fine but worth noting.
fixSteps: Add explicit Disallow for paths you don't want crawled

title: OpenAPI version in URL
description: OpenAPI / Swagger schema version is exposed in the URL.
evidence: Async / body / header check for open-api-schema-version-leak.
riskImpact: Move API version into the path prefix, not the version parameter
explanation: OpenAPI / Swagger schema version is exposed in the URL.
fixSteps: Move API version into the path prefix, not the version parameter

title: CORS exposes internal CDN
description: Access-Control-Allow-Origin includes internal CDN hostnames.
evidence: Async / body / header check for cdn-cors-exposes-internal.
riskImpact: Restrict ACAO to the customer-facing origin
explanation: Access-Control-Allow-Origin includes internal CDN hostnames.
fixSteps: Restrict ACAO to the customer-facing origin

title: reCAPTCHA site key exposure
description: Google reCAPTCHA site key is exposed in client-side code by design. Site keys are not secret.
evidence: Async / body / header check for recaptcha-key-leaked.
riskImpact: This is informational; rotate site keys if you ever suspect abuse
explanation: Google reCAPTCHA site key is exposed in client-side code by design. Site keys are not secret.
fixSteps: This is informational; rotate site keys if you ever suspect abuse

title: Google Analytics tracking ID exposed
description: GA tracking ID (UA-XXXX / G-XXXX) is publicly visible. Not a security issue by itself.
evidence: Async / body / header check for ga-tracking-id-leaked.
riskImpact: This is informational
explanation: GA tracking ID (UA-XXXX / G-XXXX) is publicly visible. Not a security issue by itself.
fixSteps: This is informational

title: nginx version disclosed in 404 / error pages
description: Default nginx error pages and the Server response header often expose the exact nginx version (e.g. 'nginx/1.18.0'). Knowing the version lets attackers map CVEs quickly.
evidence: Async / body / header check for nginx-version-404-disclosure.
riskImpact: Hide the nginx version in server_tokens off; remove or genericize the Server header.
explanation: Default nginx error pages and the Server response header often expose the exact nginx version (e.g. 'nginx/1.18.0'). Knowing the version lets attackers map CVEs quickly.
fixSteps: Add 'server_tokens off;' to nginx.conf.
Use a custom error page that does not include the server version.
Strip the Server header at the reverse proxy or CDN.

title: Apache version disclosed in 404 / error pages
description: Default Apache error pages expose the full server version, loaded modules, and OS in the footer (e.g. 'Apache/2.4.57 (CentOS) Server at example.com Port 443').
evidence: Async / body / header check for apache-version-404-disclosure.
riskImpact: Set ServerTokens Prod and ServerSignature Off in httpd.conf.
explanation: Default Apache error pages expose the full server version, loaded modules, and OS in the footer (e.g. 'Apache/2.4.57 (CentOS) Server at example.com Port 443').
fixSteps: Set 'ServerTokens Prod' to expose only 'Apache'.
Set 'ServerSignature Off' to remove the version footer.
Use a custom ErrorDocument for all 4xx/5xx codes.

title: IIS version disclosed in 404 / error pages
description: Internet Information Services (IIS) leaks its version in the Server header and in detailed error pages, including the .NET CLR version and machine name.
evidence: Async / body / header check for iis-version-404-disclosure.
riskImpact: Hide the IIS version via httpResponseHeader and disable detailed error pages.
explanation: Internet Information Services (IIS) leaks its version in the Server header and in detailed error pages, including the .NET CLR version and machine name.
fixSteps: Use URL Rewrite or web.config to remove the Server header.
Set httpErrors mode=Custom to suppress detailed errors.
Disable 'Send Detailed Error Messages' in IIS Manager.

title: Express.js default error page disclosed
description: Express in development mode renders a multi-line stack trace with file paths and line numbers. Even in production, misconfigured errorHandler() leaks the same stack.
evidence: Async / body / header check for express-error-format-disclosure.
riskImpact: Run Express with NODE_ENV=production and use a custom error handler that returns generic 500 messages.
explanation: Express in development mode renders a multi-line stack trace with file paths and line numbers. Even in production, misconfigured errorHandler() leaks the same stack.
fixSteps: Set NODE_ENV=production.
Replace app.use((err, req, res, next) => ...) with one that logs internally and returns a sanitized response.
Disable the 'x-powered-by' header with app.disable('x-powered-by').

title: Flask Werkzeug debugger PIN page exposed
description: Flask applications started with debug=True expose the interactive Werkzeug debugger, which allows arbitrary code execution via the browser console when the debugger PIN is bypassed or guessable.
evidence: Async / body / header check for flask-debug-page-exposure.
riskImpact: Disable debug mode in any environment reachable from the network.
explanation: Flask applications started with debug=True expose the interactive Werkzeug debugger, which allows arbitrary code execution via the browser console when the debugger PIN is bypassed or guessable.
fixSteps: Set debug=False (or FLASK_ENV=production) on every deploy.
Never run app.run(debug=True) in a public environment.
Wrap startup with a guard that refuses debug=True unless an explicit env flag is set.

title: Django technical 500 page exposed
description: Django renders a yellow technical 500 page with full stack traces, local variable values, and request metadata when DEBUG=True. This is intended for local development only.
evidence: Async / body / header check for django-debug-page-exposure.
riskImpact: Set DEBUG=False in production and configure a custom 500 error view.
explanation: Django renders a yellow technical 500 page with full stack traces, local variable values, and request metadata when DEBUG=True. This is intended for local development only.
fixSteps: Set DEBUG=False in settings.py for every production deploy.
Configure ADMINS and a custom 500 error view that hides internals.
Use django-environ or similar to source DEBUG from env.

title: Rails default error page disclosed
description: Rails in development mode renders the 'Welcome aboard' and detailed exception pages with source code excerpts, routes, and request parameters. In production, exceptions through ActionDispatch::DebugExceptions can still leak similar information if not configured.
evidence: Async / body / header check for rails-error-page-disclosure.
riskImpact: Run with RAILS_ENV=production and configure consider_all_requests_local=false.
explanation: Rails in development mode renders the 'Welcome aboard' and detailed exception pages with source code excerpts, routes, and request parameters. In production, exceptions through ActionDispatch::DebugExceptions can still leak similar information if not configured.
fixSteps: Set RAILS_ENV=production.
Set config.consider_all_requests_local = false in production.rb.
Configure config.action_dispatch.show_exceptions = :rescuable.

title: Spring Boot Actuator endpoint exposed
description: Spring Boot Actuator endpoints (/actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/mappings, /actuator/beans) leak environment variables, JVM heap state, and full route maps when exposed without authentication.
evidence: Async / body / header check for spring-boot-actuator-exposed.
riskImpact: Disable or strongly authenticate every Actuator endpoint; never expose /env or /heapdump on the internet.
explanation: Spring Boot Actuator endpoints (/actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/mappings, /actuator/beans) leak environment variables, JVM heap state, and full route maps when exposed without authentication.
fixSteps: Set management.endpoints.web.exposure.include to a minimal allowlist.
Place Actuator on a separate management port bound to localhost only.
Require authentication (Spring Security) for any Actuator endpoint.

title: Jenkins version disclosed in headers / login page
description: Jenkins exposes its exact version in the X-Jenkins response header and on the login page footer. Combined with the open script console, this is a high-value fingerprint.
evidence: Async / body / header check for jenkins-version-exposure.
riskImpact: Hide the Jenkins version via the System Property 'hudson.diyChunking' or by fronting with an authenticating reverse proxy.
explanation: Jenkins exposes its exact version in the X-Jenkins response header and on the login page footer. Combined with the open script console, this is a high-value fingerprint.
fixSteps: Set System.setProperty('hudson.diyChunking', 'true') and configure a frontend that strips X-Jenkins.
Require authentication on every Jenkins route.
Front Jenkins with a reverse proxy that adds its own security headers.

title: Grafana version disclosed
description: Grafana discloses its exact version in the X-Grafana-Version response header on every request. Knowing the version lets attackers map CVEs (e.g. CVE-2024-9264).
evidence: Async / body / header check for grafana-version-exposure.
riskImpact: Place Grafana behind an authenticating reverse proxy that strips the X-Grafana-Version header.
explanation: Grafana discloses its exact version in the X-Grafana-Version response header on every request. Knowing the version lets attackers map CVEs (e.g. CVE-2024-9264).
fixSteps: Front Grafana with a reverse proxy that strips the X-Grafana-Version header.
Require authentication on /login and /api/health.
Subscribe to Grafana security advisories and patch promptly.

title: Next.js 13+ App Router detected via RSC headers
description: Next.js 13+ App Router identifies itself via the RSC (React Server Components) header (RSC: 1, Next-Router-State-Tree) on every page response. This is informational but useful for fingerprinting.
evidence: Async / body / header check for nextjs-app-router-rsc-headers.
riskImpact: No fix required, but be aware that fingerprinting reveals the framework version line; ensure all framework-level CVEs are patched.
explanation: Next.js 13+ App Router identifies itself via the RSC (React Server Components) header (RSC: 1, Next-Router-State-Tree) on every page response. This is informational but useful for fingerprinting.
fixSteps: Keep Next.js patched to the latest minor release.
Front the app with a CDN that standardizes response headers.

title: SvelteKit framework detected via x-sveltekit-* headers
description: SvelteKit responses include x-sveltekit-* debug headers and a __data.json preload convention that fingerprints the framework.
evidence: Async / body / header check for sveltekit-detection.
riskImpact: No fix required; keep SvelteKit updated and audit data-loading endpoints.
explanation: SvelteKit responses include x-sveltekit-* debug headers and a __data.json preload convention that fingerprints the framework.
fixSteps: Update SvelteKit regularly.
Strip debug headers at the reverse proxy if exposing them is undesirable.

title: Vite dev client exposed in production
description: Vite serves @vite/client and module hot-reload scripts at /@vite/client when running in development. Finding these in a production build indicates the dev server was accidentally exposed.
evidence: Async / body / header check for vite-client-exposed.
riskImpact: Stop serving the Vite dev server; build with 'vite build' and serve the dist/ output from a static host.
explanation: Vite serves @vite/client and module hot-reload scripts at /@vite/client when running in development. Finding these in a production build indicates the dev server was accidentally exposed.
fixSteps: Run 'vite build' and serve the dist/ folder from a static-only host (nginx, S3+CloudFront).
Never expose 'vite' or 'vite preview' to the public internet.
Add a CSP that disallows inline scripts to mitigate live-reload code execution.

title: AWS S3 NoSuchBucket error pattern exposed
description: S3 bucket hosting exposes 'NoSuchBucket' or 'AccessDenied' XML error responses that reveal the bucket name, region, and AWS account ID via the x-amz-request-id header.
evidence: Async / body / header check for aws-s3-nosuchbucket-error.
riskImpact: Genericize S3 error responses with a CDN/WAF and avoid linking directly to bucket hostnames.
explanation: S3 bucket hosting exposes 'NoSuchBucket' or 'AccessDenied' XML error responses that reveal the bucket name, region, and AWS account ID via the x-amz-request-id header.
fixSteps: Front S3 with CloudFront or another CDN that rewrites error pages.
Disable direct bucket hostname access via bucket policies.
Use a vanity domain for S3-backed static sites.

title: MySQL 'Access denied' error pattern exposed
description: Pages exposing 'Access denied for user 'X'@'Y' (using password: YES/NO)' leak the MySQL username, client host, and password status. Attackers use this to target MySQL brute-force and credential-stuffing lists.
evidence: Async / body / header check for mysql-access-denied-error.
riskImpact: Hide MySQL errors behind a custom error handler; never echo raw DB errors to clients.
explanation: Pages exposing 'Access denied for user 'X'@'Y' (using password: YES/NO)' leak the MySQL username, client host, and password status. Attackers use this to target MySQL brute-force and credential-stuffing lists.
fixSteps: Catch PDOException / mysql_error() in the application layer and return a generic 500.
Rotate any credentials that have appeared in a leaked error.
Restrict DB user privileges and restrict client hosts via GRANT ... TO 'user'@'host'.
